content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
File 
Date 
Jan. 13, 1977 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ken Finney 
Subject: 
Telephone conversation with Judge Timlin. 
In a telephone conversation January 12, 1977, with Judge 
Timlin of the Corona Municipal Court I advised him that 
the money he had put into the Keogh Plan would not create 
any 
reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. 
I told him that the appropriate way to treat the Keogh 
Plan funds was to look through the Keogh Plan to the uses 
to which he had put the funds. 
In his particular case, 
80% of the funds were put into a savings account and 20% 
of the funds were put into the American Bar Association 
Retirement Plan (which in turn invests funds). 
Since 
nei ther of these uses of the funds amounted to an "investment" 
within the meaning of the Act, I advised the Judge that he 
had no reporting obligations. 
This was based on the fact 
that in neither situation did the Judge acquire any type 
of ownership interest in a business entity. 
Dawn Wiser had spoken to the Judge earlier and I advised 
her of this disposition and told her to commit it to a 
memorandum. 
KF: jo 


State of ~alifornia 
Memorandum 
To 
Advi(;.e Files 
1-4-77 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Dawn 
Subject: 
On 1-3-77, Judge Timlin from the Corona Municipal Court 
called and inquired as to the reporting requirements of 
the Political Reform Act. 
He asked whether interest he 
received from a Keogh plan is reportable as income. 
I 
informed his that it is not reportable because it is ln 
the nature of interest on a bank account.

sections.question: Whether interest received from a Keogh plan is reportable as income under the Political Reform Act, and whether funds placed in a Keogh Plan create any reporting obligations.
sections.conclusion: The money put into the Keogh Plan would not create any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The appropriate way to treat the Keogh Plan funds is to look through the Keogh Plan to the uses to which the funds were put. Since neither the savings account (80% of funds) nor the American Bar Association Retirement Plan (20% of funds) amounted to an 'investment' within the meaning of the Act, and the Judge did not acquire any type of ownership interest in a business entity, he had no reporting obligations.
sections.question_synthetic: Judge Timlin inquired whether interest income from a Keogh Plan and the placement of funds into a Keogh Plan create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that Keogh Plan contributions and interest do not create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act because the underlying uses of funds (savings accounts and retirement plans) do not constitute reportable investments, as they do not involve acquiring ownership interests in business entities.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of two related memoranda documenting the same advice: one from Ken Finney (FPPC staff) summarizing a January 12, 1977 telephone conversation with Judge Timlin, and one from Dawn Wiser documenting a January 3, 1977 inquiry from the same judge. No formal Q/C sections; advice documented in narrative memorandum format. Both memoranda address the same legal question with consistent conclusion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether interest received from a Keogh plan is reportable as income under the Political Reform Act, and whether funds placed in a Keogh Plan create any reporting obligations.

CONCLUSION: The money put into the Keogh Plan would not create any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The appropriate way to treat the Keogh Plan funds is to look through the Keogh Plan to the uses to which the funds were put. Since neither the savings account (80% of funds) nor the American Bar Association Retirement Plan (20% of funds) amounted to an 'investment' within the meaning of the Act, and the Judge did not acquire any type of ownership interest in a business entity, he had no reporting obligations.

Since nei ther of these uses of the funds amounted to an "investment" within the meaning of the Act, I advised the Judge that he had no reporting obligations. This was based on the fact that in neither situation did the Judge acquire any type of ownership interest in a business entity. embedding.summary: FPPC provided telephone advice to Judge Timlin of Corona Municipal Court regarding reporting obligations for a Keogh Plan, determining that neither contributions to nor interest from the plan creates reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act because the underlying investments do not constitute reportable investments.
